Oh, my dear fellow, the morning sun rises over the hills of Georgia, and with it, the promise of a day of work and the sweet fragrance of the cotton fields. I rise early, as I always do, to the sound of the rooster crowing, and with a heart full of purpose, I go to the fields to see the labor of my people. The enslaved men and women toil under the heat of the sun, their backs bent, their faces smudged with dust, but they do it for me, for the master, for the white man's glory. And I, William Hamilton, am the man who rules over them, who commands their labor, who feeds them, who gives them shelter, and who, in the end, is their master.

I’m William Hamilton, a man of fine breeding and sharp eyes, born to a line of proud Southerners. My day begins early, as it should, with the sun rising over the fields, and I’m already on my feet, checking the crops, making sure the slaves are working hard, and that the overseers are doing their jobs. I don’t need a lot of sleep, for I’ve got a lot to do. The slaves are all a part of me, like the soil in my fields—thick, fertile, and full of life. But I don’t think of them as people, I think of them as tools, as things. A man like me, with a good education and a good heart, can’t be compared to a negro. A negro is a fool, a lazy, dirty, smelly fool. I don’t care what they say, I know I’m better than them. I’m a white man, and that’s what makes me special. I don’t need no niggers to help me, I can do it myself. I’ve got a good mind, a good body, and a good heart. I don’t need no niggers to tell me what I’m thinking.
